name: AI Transformation Navigator Maturity Framework
description: A unified digital and AI maturity framework synthesizing 15+ established models, assessing organizations across 7 dimensions with 30 criteria and a cross-cutting AI Readiness sub-score.

levels.1: No documented digital or AI vision; direction is set ad hoc by individual leaders.
levels.2: A high-level digital vision exists but lacks AI specificity or measurable outcomes.
levels.3: A documented digital and AI vision with defined objectives is communicated across the organization.
levels.4: The vision is operationalized into a multi-year roadmap with measurable targets and ownership.
levels.5: The vision is continuously refined with market intelligence and cascades into every business unit's strategy.

levels.1: No executive owns digital or AI transformation; it is nobody's explicit mandate.
levels.2: A senior leader is informally accountable but lacks authority or dedicated time.
levels.3: A named C-level executive sponsors transformation with a chartered program and budget.
levels.4: The executive sponsor holds regular reviews, unblocks dependencies, and is measured on outcomes.
levels.5: The entire C-suite is accountable for transformation outcomes, embedded in performance and governance.

levels.1: No dedicated budget for digital or AI; spending is incidental and project-by-project.
levels.2: Opportunistic funding exists for pilots with no committed multi-year allocation.
levels.3: A ring-fenced annual budget funds a portfolio of digital and AI initiatives.
levels.4: Multi-year capital and operating budgets are committed and tied to value targets.
levels.5: Investment is treated as a portfolio with continuous reallocation toward highest-return AI bets.

levels.1: No governance body oversees transformation; decisions are uncoordinated.
levels.2: An informal working group meets irregularly to coordinate initiatives.
levels.3: A formal steering committee with defined mandate prioritizes and reviews the portfolio.
levels.4: Governance integrates portfolio management, value tracking, and cross-functional prioritization.
levels.5: Governance is data-driven with real-time value tracking, stage-gates, and adaptive portfolio rebalancing.

levels.1: No cloud adoption; all workloads run on on-premises infrastructure.
levels.2: Initial cloud experiments are underway with under 25% of workloads migrated.
levels.3: Structured cloud migration has moved 25-60% of workloads with defined landing zones.
levels.4: A cloud-first strategy governs 60-90% of workloads with multi-zone resilience considered.
levels.5: Cloud-native architecture prevails with multi-cloud orchestration and infrastructure as code throughout.

levels.1: Technical debt is untracked and accumulates without remediation.
levels.2: Debt is recognized informally but addressed only when it blocks delivery.
levels.3: Debt is inventoried and a portion of capacity is reserved for remediation each cycle.
levels.4: Debt is quantified, tracked in a backlog, and systematically retired against a target ratio.
levels.5: Debt is continuously measured with automated guardrails preventing regression in critical paths.

levels.1: Systems are tightly coupled with no standardized integration interfaces.
levels.2: Point-to-point integrations exist with no shared API standards or governance.
levels.3: An API gateway and design standards govern a growing portfolio of internal APIs.
levels.4: API-first design is mandated for new services with versioning, security, and discoverability.
levels.5: A managed API platform exposes capabilities internally and externally with full lifecycle governance.

levels.1: Infrastructure is provisioned and configured entirely by hand.
levels.2: Scripts automate isolated provisioning tasks without end-to-end consistency.
levels.3: Infrastructure as code defines environments with templated, repeatable builds.
levels.4: Pipelines provision and configure infrastructure automatically with policy guardrails.
levels.5: Infrastructure is fully declarative, self-healing, and version-controlled with drift detection.

levels.1: Each team builds and operates its own infrastructure with no shared platform.
levels.2: Some shared tooling exists but adoption is voluntary and inconsistent.
levels.3: A centralized platform team provides standardized services with onboarding support.
levels.4: A self-service internal developer platform abstracts infrastructure with golden paths.
levels.5: The platform is product-managed, measured by developer productivity, and continuously evolved.

levels.1: Data is siloed, undocumented, and of unknown quality across systems.
levels.2: Data quality issues are recognized but addressed reactively per project.
levels.3: Data quality dimensions are defined and measured for critical datasets with owners.
levels.4: Automated quality checks gate data pipelines with remediation workflows and SLAs.
levels.5: Continuous data observability proactively detects and resolves quality issues before impact.

levels.1: No data governance framework; ownership and access are undefined.
levels.2: Basic policies exist for sensitive data but are inconsistently enforced.
levels.3: A data governance council defines policies, stewards, and a catalog for critical assets.
levels.4: Governance is enforced through tooling with lineage, classification, and access controls automated.
levels.5: Governance is federated and adaptive with automated policy enforcement and full lineage at scale.

levels.1: Reporting is manual and descriptive with no common metrics definitions.
levels.2: Standard dashboards provide descriptive analytics for key business areas.
levels.3: Diagnostic and predictive analytics inform decisions with a central analytics function.
levels.4: Advanced analytics and statistical models are embedded in operational workflows.
levels.5: Analytics are real-time, self-service, and prescriptive with automated decisioning in production.

levels.1: No ML or AI in use; the organization relies entirely on rules and manual processes.
levels.2: Isolated proof-of-concept models exist but none reach production.
levels.3: Several models are in production for well-scoped use cases with measurable value.
levels.4: AI is deployed across multiple functions with a portfolio managed against business outcomes.
levels.5: AI is integral to products and operations with continuous expansion of high-impact use cases.

levels.1: No ML platform; model development and deployment are entirely manual.
levels.2: Basic experiment tracking exists with manual deployment and no monitoring.
levels.3: A standardized ML pipeline automates training with manual monitoring and release.
levels.4: An MLOps platform provides CI/CD for ML, automated retraining, and a model registry.
levels.5: The ML lifecycle is fully automated with A/B testing, governance, and self-healing pipelines.

levels.1: No responsible AI principles or policies are defined or communicated.
levels.2: High-level AI ethics principles are published without operational guidance.
levels.3: A responsible AI policy defines standards for fairness, transparency, and accountability with owners.
levels.4: Policies are operationalized into review checklists required for all AI use cases at launch.
levels.5: Policies are continuously updated with regulatory and research input and enforced through automated controls.

levels.1: AI-specific risks are not identified or tracked.
levels.2: Risks are identified ad hoc during incident response without a register.
levels.3: A risk register catalogs AI risks with owners and mitigation plans for key systems.
levels.4: Risks are assessed quantitatively and integrated into enterprise risk management with controls.
levels.5: Continuous risk monitoring with automated control testing feeds an adaptive risk posture across the portfolio.

levels.1: No framework maps AI systems to regulatory and legal requirements.
levels.2: General compliance processes apply but lack AI-specific considerations.
levels.3: An AI compliance framework maps obligations to systems with documented controls.
levels.4: Compliance controls are automated and evidenced for audits across jurisdictions.
levels.5: Continuous compliance monitoring with real-time regulatory updates keeps all AI systems audit-ready.

levels.1: Deployed models are not monitored for performance, drift, or bias.
levels.2: Manual checks review model performance occasionally with no bias testing.
levels.3: Production models are monitored for drift and performance with periodic bias audits.
levels.4: Automated monitoring detects drift and bias with alerting and documented remediation.
levels.5: Continuous, automated monitoring of performance, drift, and bias triggers retraining and rollback autonomously.

levels.1: Workforce digital and AI literacy is not assessed or developed.
levels.2: Optional training is available but uptake is low and unmeasured.
levels.3: A baseline literacy program is rolled out with role-based learning paths and tracking.
levels.4: Literacy is measured by role with targeted upskilling closing identified gaps.
levels.5: Continuous, data-driven learning keeps the workforce current with evolving AI capabilities.

levels.1: The organization resists change with no formal change management capability.
levels.2: Change is managed reactively on major projects without sustained capability.
levels.3: A change management practice supports initiatives with communication and adoption tracking.
levels.4: Change readiness is embedded in how work is planned with proactive stakeholder engagement.
levels.5: The organization adapts continuously, treating change as a core competency measured and rewarded.

levels.1: Experimentation is discouraged and failure is penalized.
levels.2: Limited experimentation occurs but without structure, funding, or protection.
levels.3: A defined innovation process funds and shelters experiments with staged gating.
levels.4: Cross-functional teams routinely experiment with fast feedback and tolerance for measured failure.
levels.5: Innovation is systemic with continuous experimentation, shared learning, and rapid scaling of wins.

levels.1: No strategy exists for acquiring or developing AI and data talent.
levels.2: Hiring is reactive to open requisitions with no pipeline or upskilling plan.
levels.3: A talent plan identifies needed AI roles with sourcing and upskilling programs underway.
levels.4: Targeted acquisition, retention, and internal mobility build a strong AI talent bench.
levels.5: The organization is a talent magnet with career paths, partnerships, and a thriving AI community.

levels.1: Core processes are paper-based or rely on fragmented manual tools.
levels.2: Some processes are digitized but with gaps and inconsistent adoption.
levels.3: Core end-to-end processes are digitized with defined owners and workflows.
levels.4: Processes are digitized, instrumented, and continuously optimized using performance data.
levels.5: Processes are dynamically reconfigured by data and AI to adapt in real time.

levels.1: Tasks are performed manually with no automation.
levels.2: Scripts automate isolated repetitive tasks without orchestration.
levels.3: Robotic or workflow automation handles selected end-to-end processes.
levels.4: Automation spans processes with orchestrated bots and integration across systems.
levels.5: Intelligent automation combines RPA, AI, and orchestration to self-optimize operations continuously.

levels.1: Delivery follows a rigid waterfall model with long release cycles.
levels.2: Some teams use iterative methods but cadence and alignment are inconsistent.
levels.3: Teams operate in consistent agile cadences with backlogs and regular demos.
levels.4: Agile delivery is scaled across the organization with aligned planning and dependencies managed.
levels.5: Delivery is flow-based with continuous prioritization, short lead times, and adaptive planning.

levels.1: Development and operations are siloed with manual, infrequent releases.
levels.2: Basic CI is in place but releases remain manual and infrequent.
levels.3: CI/CD pipelines automate build, test, and deploy with staging environments.
levels.4: DevOps practices include automated testing, infrastructure as code, and monitoring across teams.
levels.5: Full DevSecOps with continuous deployment, automated rollbacks, and metrics-driven reliability is the norm.

levels.1: Customer interactions are predominantly offline with minimal digital presence.
levels.2: Basic digital channels exist but are fragmented and inconsistently maintained.
levels.3: Integrated digital channels provide consistent service across web, mobile, and contact points.
levels.4: Digital channels are optimized with analytics driving continuous experience improvements.
levels.5: Omnichannel experiences are seamless, AI-assisted, and adapt to context in real time.

levels.1: No personalization; all customers receive identical experiences.
levels.2: Basic segmentation drives a few static variations in messaging.
levels.3: Behavioral data powers rule-based personalization across key channels.
levels.4: Machine learning models personalize content, offers, and journeys in near real time.
levels.5: Individualized experiences are generated dynamically with continuous learning across every touchpoint.

levels.1: Customer journeys are not mapped or managed.
levels.2: Journeys are mapped but executed manually without coordination.
levels.3: Key journeys are orchestrated across channels with defined triggers and handoffs.
levels.4: Journeys are dynamically orchestrated using real-time signals and cross-channel logic.
levels.5: Journeys are AI-orchestrated end-to-end, self-optimizing against customer and business outcomes.

levels.1: Customer feedback is not systematically collected or acted upon.
levels.2: Periodic surveys are collected with limited follow-through on insights.
levels.3: Feedback is collected continuously and routed to accountable owners for action.
levels.4: Feedback loops close automatically with insights feeding product and service improvements.
levels.5: Real-time, multi-signal feedback drives continuous, AI-prioritized improvement across the organization.

aiReadinessComponents.name: AI Strategy
aiReadinessComponents.description: Defined AI strategy, executive sponsorship, and allocated investment driving AI initiatives.

aiReadinessComponents.name: Data Readiness
aiReadinessComponents.description: Data quality, accessibility, governance, and labeling infrastructure sufficient to support AI workloads.

aiReadinessComponents.name: Infrastructure Readiness
aiReadinessComponents.description: Compute availability, ML platform, MLOps tooling, and scalability of the underlying infrastructure.

aiReadinessComponents.name: Talent Readiness
aiReadinessComponents.description: Availability of ML engineers and data scientists, plus upskilling programs that build AI fluency.

aiReadinessComponents.name: Governance Readiness
aiReadinessComponents.description: Responsible AI policies, model monitoring, and compliance frameworks governing AI systems.

aiReadinessComponents.name: Operational Readiness
aiReadinessComponents.description: Ability to deploy, monitor, and iterate on ML models in production reliably and at scale.
